common_name: Ionantha Air Plant (Blushing Bride Tillandsia, Sky Plant)

planting.note: Grow mounted on cork bark, driftwood, wire, or shells in bright indirect to direct morning light; Ionantha Air Plant needs no soil whatsoever. Water by dunking in water for 20–30 minutes twice per week in warm months or misting heavily every 2–3 days. After watering, shake off excess water and allow to dry completely within 4 hours — sitting in stagnant water causes rot at the base. A good indirect air source prevents this. In zone 9, plants can grow outdoors year-round in protected spots and survive brief temperatures to 32°F dry. Produces offsets (pups) after blooming.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to gentle direct morning sun — Ionantha Air Plant performs best with 3–4 hours of bright indirect or morning sun and will bleach and dry out in harsh full afternoon sun in zone 9. Tillandsia ionantha is a small, epiphytic bromeliad native to Mexico, Guatemala, Honduras, Nicaragua, and northwestern Venezuela in the family Bromeliaceae, one of the approximately 650 species of Tillandsia and by far the most widely grown and sold air plant in the world. Like all tillandsias, it has no functional roots — the small roots serve only to anchor the plant to its substrate. All water and nutrients are absorbed through specialized scales on the leaf surfaces called trichomes. The plant forms a small, tight rosette of silvery-green, slightly recurved, stiff leaves about 2–3 inches across. Its most dramatic quality is its response to blooming: in spring (or when seasonal cues trigger reproduction), the inner leaves of the entire plant flush brilliant red to rose-magenta over a period of days before the small violet-purple tubular flowers emerge from the center — the entire plant glowing red for weeks. After flowering, the parent plant gradually produces 2–6 pups before declining. The combination of tiny size, no soil requirement, dramatic bloom-time color, and extreme adaptability has made T. ionantha the definitive entry point for air plant collecting.
sources: Bromeliad Society International — Tillandsia ionantha cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tillandsia species for Florida
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Air plants for Gulf Coast landscapes
American Orchid Society — Bromeliad air plant culture

common_name: Xerographica Air Plant (King of Air Plants, Silver Ball Tillandsia)

planting.note: Display on open cork board, in a bowl, or mounted in bright direct to indirect light; Tillandsia xerographica is native to dry tropical forests (the specific epithet means 'dry writing') and is significantly more drought-tolerant than most tillandsias. Water by misting heavily once per week in summer or dunking briefly for 5–10 minutes monthly — overwatering is the primary killer. Allow to dry within 4 hours. In zone 9, it can be grown outdoors year-round in exposed, bright spots and tolerates temperatures to 32°F when dry. One of the slowest-growing tillandsias — a large silver ball represents many years of growth.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to direct morning sun — Tillandsia xerographica is more sun-tolerant than most tillandsias and actually requires brighter light than T. ionantha and other small species to look its best; it will become loose and etiolated in inadequate light. Tillandsia xerographica is a large, slow-growing, epiphytic bromeliad native to the semi-arid tropical dry forests of Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, and southern Mexico, listed as a protected species in CITES Appendix II due to over-collection from the wild. It has been called the 'King of Air Plants' — a title that reflects both its commanding size (a mature specimen forms a rounded silver ball 12–30 inches across) and its dramatic appearance. The long (12–24 inch), silvery-white to pale gray, curling, strap-like leaves spiral outward and curl back at their tips in a loose, sculptural, spherical arrangement that is among the most architecturally elegant of all bromeliads. The silver color comes from the dense coating of trichomes that cover every leaf surface, maximizing atmospheric moisture absorption from the dry habitats where the plant grows. In spring or summer, a tall flower spike rises from the center producing an inflorescence of pink, green, and purple bracts surrounding small violet tubular flowers. Tillandsia xerographica is extraordinarily long-lived and slow-growing — a prized, prestigious specimen plant for distinctive decorating.
sources: Bromeliad Society International — Tillandsia xerographica: King of Air Plants
CITES Appendix II — Tillandsia xerographica (protected species)
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tillandsia species for indoor use
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Large air plants for zone 9-10 Gulf Coast

common_name: Earth Star Bromeliad (Starfish Plant, Bi-banded Cryptanthus)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant, terrarium plant, or shaded patio container in zone 9; Earth Star Bromeliad cannot tolerate frost. Unlike most bromeliads, it has no central water cup and grows directly in the soil — use a well-draining, peat or coir-based mix. Unlike the tank bromeliads, water it through the soil like a regular potted plant. High humidity (50–70%) and consistent warmth improve appearance. Excellent for terrariums where humidity is maintained naturally. Propagate by removing pups from the base after the main plant flowers. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Earth Star Bromeliad is one of the most shade-tolerant bromeliads, maintaining its attractive appearance and best color in conditions of moderate indirect light; intense direct sun bleaches the coloration. Cryptanthus bivittatus is a small, flat, terrestrial bromeliad native to Brazil in the family Bromeliaceae, one of approximately 70 species in the genus Cryptanthus (the only bromeliad genus that is entirely terrestrial — all other bromeliads are at least partially epiphytic). The plant produces a flat, star-shaped (hence 'Earth Star') rosette of narrow, stiff, slightly spiny-edged leaves that are wavy and spread horizontally just above the soil surface. In the species and common cultivars, the leaves are marked with two lengthwise stripes (the 'bivittatus' — 'two-banded') of cream-white or pink against a bronze-green to reddish-brown background — but the hundreds of Cryptanthus hybrids and cultivars available today cover an extraordinary range of colors, stripes, waves, and textures in red, pink, purple, bronze, copper, cream, green, and multicolor combinations, making the genus among the most colorful in the bromeliad family. Small, inconspicuous white flowers appear in the center. Unlike the tank bromeliads, Earth Stars absorb water through their roots rather than a central cup, making them uniquely suited to terrarium culture. Non-toxic.
sources: Bromeliad Society International — Cryptanthus (Earth Star) cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cryptanthus species for indoor use
American Bromeliad Society — Cryptanthus bivittatus care guide
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Cryptanthus (non-toxic)

common_name: Noble Dendrobium (Nobile Orchid, Cane Orchid, Spring Orchid)

planting.note: Grow in a slatted orchid pot or basket with coarse bark media in bright indirect to partial direct light; Dendrobium nobile is one of the more cold-tolerant orchids, surviving temperatures to 40°F if kept dry — its Himalayan origin predisposes it to cool winters. In zone 9, grow on a shaded porch or bright indoor windowsill. CRITICAL for blooming: withhold water almost completely (barely misting monthly) for 6–8 weeks in fall when nights cool to 50–55°F — this cool-dry rest is essential for flower bud development. After rest period, resume normal watering to trigger bud development. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to gentle direct morning sun — Noble Dendrobium blooms most prolifically in good light with an unobstructed east or shaded south exposure; it will survive but rarely bloom in dim conditions. Dendrobium nobile is a sympodial, cane-forming epiphytic orchid native to the Himalayan foothills of India (Assam, Sikkim, Meghalaya), Nepal, Bhutan, southern China, Laos, Thailand, and Vietnam, growing at elevations of 2,000–5,000 feet in a climate with cool, dry winters and warm, wet summers. The plant produces upright, cane-like pseudobulbs (stems) 12–24 inches tall clothed in leaves that are sometimes deciduous in winter. In late winter and spring, after the essential cool-dry rest period, flower buds emerge from the nodes along the previous year's canes and open to clusters of 3–5 flowers per node, covering the entire length of each cane — in a well-bloomed specimen, dozens of clusters may open simultaneously. The flowers are large (2–3 inches), waxy, fragrant, in shades of white to lavender-purple with a dark purple center in the lip — a classically beautiful orchid flower. Dendrobium nobile is the parent species of the large Dendrobium nobile hybrid orchids widely sold as gift plants in spring, which share the clustered-blooming habit. More cold-tolerant than most popular orchids. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Dendrobium nobile cultivation guide
Royal Horticultural Society — Dendrobium nobile cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dendrobium orchids for indoor culture
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Dendrobium (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Sacred Fig (Bodhi Tree, Peepal Tree, Bo Tree)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in full sun to partial shade with well-drained soil; Ficus religiosa is frost-sensitive and best suited to zone 10-11 for outdoor planting. In zone 9, it can be grown as a large container specimen or bonsai. Keep away from structures — the roots and canopy are powerfully invasive. Unlike most figs, it is semi-deciduous, briefly dropping its distinctive heart-shaped leaves before quickly producing new ones. The milky sap irritates skin.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Sacred Fig grows vigorously in tropical and subtropical conditions and should be sited away from all structures, pipes, and paving. Ficus religiosa is a large, semi-deciduous fig tree native to the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia, one of the most sacred plants in human history. It is the tree under which Siddhartha Gautama achieved enlightenment, becoming the Buddha, at Bodh Gaya in present-day India — an event considered so significant that the tree, and its descendants propagated from that original specimen, are venerated throughout the Buddhist world. The plant is equally sacred in Hinduism and Jainism. Its distinctive heart-shaped to ovate leaves have an extraordinarily elongated drip tip — sometimes 2–3 times the length of the leaf blade — that evolved to channel water rapidly off the leaf surface in the heavy monsoon rains of its native habitat. These trembling, long-tipped leaves create a distinctive rustling sound in the slightest breeze, a quality noted in both botanical and spiritual literature. In zone 10-11 Gulf Coast and Florida, Sacred Fig is grown as a significant landscape specimen and street tree. In zone 9, it is best maintained as a large container tree or bonsai subject, moved to protected locations in winter. Small, inedible figs are produced and consumed by birds.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ficus religiosa (Sacred Fig)
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Ficus religiosa cultivation notes
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Ficus religiosa species profile
Bodh Gaya Temple Management Committee — Historical documentation of the original Bodhi Tree
